key_points: Work is the product of force and displacement in the direction of force: W = F × d × cosθ.
Positive work: force and displacement in same direction; Negative work: opposite directions; Zero work: displacement perpendicular to force.
Kinetic energy (KE) depends on mass and velocity: KE = 1/2 mv².
Potential energy (PE) depends on mass and height: PE = mgh.
Elastic potential energy stored in springs: PE_spring = 1/2 k x².
Work-energy theorem: Work done = change in kinetic energy, W = ΔKE.
Law of conservation of energy: Total energy in an isolated system remains constant.
Power is the rate of doing work: P = W / t.
Efficiency of a machine: Efficiency = (Useful work output / Total work input) × 100%.
formulas: Elastic potential energy: PE_spring = 1/2 k x²
Work-energy theorem: W = ΔKE = KE_final − KE_initial
Efficiency: Efficiency (%) = (Useful work output / Total work input) × 100%
examples: A 3 kg body moving at 4 m/s: KE = 1/2 × 3 × 4² = 24 J
A 5 kg mass lifted 8 m: PE = 5 × 9.8 × 8 = 392 J
Spring stretched 0.2 m with k = 100 N/m: PE_spring = 1/2 × 100 × 0.2² = 2 J
Force of 12 N moves body 6 m at 30° to displacement: W = 12 × 6 × cos30° ≈ 62.4 J
Machine uses 500 J input energy, does 400 J useful work → Efficiency = (400/500)×100 = 80%
exam_tips: Always resolve forces along the displacement for work calculations.
Use W = ΔKE when force and motion are variable.
For inclined planes, use energy conservation: PE_top = KE_bottom.
Check angles carefully: cosθ affects work value.
Draw energy bar diagrams for combined KE and PE problems.
